questions.question:
Select the graph that schematically represents BOTH y = x^m and y = x^(1/m) properly in the interval 0 <= x <= 1, for integer values of m, where m > 1.

A. [Graph A: y = x^(1/m) is the upper curve (bows upward) and y = x^m is the lower curve (bows downward), both passing through (0,0) and (1,1)]
B. [Graph B: y = x^m is the upper curve and y = x^(1/m) is the lower curve]
C. [Graph C: y = x^m is the upper curve and y = x^(1/m) is the lower curve, curves close together]
D. [Graph D: y = x^(1/m) is the upper curve but both curves appear concave toward the origin]
questions.answer: A. y = x^(1/m) above y = x^m on (0,1)
questions.to_find: which graph correctly shows both y = x^m and y = x^(1/m) on the interval [0, 1] for m > 1

understand.plain: For m > 1 and x in (0, 1), raising x to a large power (x^m) makes it smaller, while taking a fractional root (x^(1/m)) makes it larger. Both curves pass through (0,0) and (1,1). The curve x^(1/m) lies above the diagonal y = x and x^m lies below it in the open interval (0,1). So x^(1/m) is always above x^m for x in (0,1) when m > 1.

keywords.explain: Raising a number between 0 and 1 to a power greater than 1 makes it smaller (e.g., 0.5^2 = 0.25). The curve bows toward the x-axis.
Taking a fractional root of a number between 0 and 1 makes it larger (e.g., sqrt(0.25) = 0.5). The curve bows toward the y-axis.
y = x^(1/m) is the inverse function of y = x^m, so their graphs are reflections of each other across the diagonal y = x. This means they are on opposite sides of the diagonal.
keywords.example: If (a, b) is on y = x^m, then (b, a) is on y = x^(1/m)

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='260' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Behaviour of x^m vs x^(1/m) on [0,1]</text><rect x='40' y='35' width='180' height='180' fill='white' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='215' x2='220' y2='215' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='215' x2='40' y2='35' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='36' y='218' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='218' y='230' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='40' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='228' y='218' class='sub ink'>x</text><text x='33' y='40' class='sub ink'>y</text><line x1='40' y1='215' x2='220' y2='35' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='225' y='38' class='sub mut'>y=x</text><path d='M40,215 Q110,180 220,35' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><text x='105' y='185' class='tag no'>x^m</text><path d='M40,215 Q80,60 220,35' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><text x='60' y='110' class='tag ok'>x^(1/m)</text><text x='250' y='90' class='sub ok'>x^(1/m)</text><text x='250' y='105' class='sub ink'>is above</text><text x='250' y='120' class='sub no'>x^m</text><text x='250' y='135' class='sub ink'>on (0,1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A coordinate plot showing x^(1/m) bowing toward the y-axis (upper curve, green) and x^m bowing toward the x-axis (lower curve, red) in the unit square, with both curves meeting at (0,0) and (1,1).

given.aim: identify the correct schematic graph showing both y = x^m and y = x^(1/m) on 0 <= x <= 1 for integer m > 1

terms.meaning: power function with integer exponent m > 1; for x in (0,1) this gives values less than x, curving toward the x-axis
terms.connects: lower curve in the unit square

terms.meaning: root function (m-th root); for x in (0,1) this gives values greater than x, curving toward the y-axis
terms.connects: upper curve in the unit square

terms.meaning: at x=0, both give y=0; at x=1, both give y=1, so both curves share the same endpoints
terms.connects: anchors the curves at the corners of the unit square

terms.meaning: y = x^(1/m) is the inverse of y = x^m, so their graphs are mirror images across y = x
terms.connects: explains why the two curves are symmetric about the diagonal y = x

given.plan: Test a specific value (e.g. x = 0.5, m = 2) to determine which curve is above the other in (0,1): x^m = 0.25 < 0.5 < 0.5^(1/2) = 0.707. So x^(1/m) is above x^m. The correct graph must show x^(1/m) as the upper (concave downward from diagonal) curve and x^m as the lower (concave upward from diagonal) curve.

steps.title: Test a specific value to compare x^m and x^(1/m)
steps.formula_raw: x^m vs x^(1/m) at x=0.5, m=2
steps.note: This confirms x^(1/m) lies strictly above x^m for all x in the open interval (0, 1).

steps.title: Verify the general inequality for all x in (0,1)
steps.formula_raw: For 0 < x < 1 and m > 1: x^m < x < x^(1/m)
steps.apply: Since m > 1, the exponent m makes the value smaller (x^m < x) and the exponent 1/m makes the value larger (x^(1/m) > x). So the order is x^m < x < x^(1/m) for all x in (0,1).
steps.note: Both curves pass through (0,0) and (1,1), so the relative positions hold only in the open interval.

steps.title: Match to the correct graph
steps.apply: The correct graph must show: x^(1/m) as the upper curve (bowing toward the y-axis / concave toward origin) and x^m as the lower curve (bowing toward the x-axis). Option A is the only graph with x^(1/m) above x^m in this correct configuration.
steps.note: Options B and C incorrectly swap the curves. Option D places both curves incorrectly.

solution.result: Option A is correct: x^(1/m) is the upper curve and x^m is the lower curve in [0, 1] for m > 1.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula applies; the result follows from the monotonicity of the exponential function and direct numerical comparison.

questions.question:
P, Q, R and S are four types of dangerous microbes recently found in a human habitat. The area of each circle with its diameter printed in brackets represents the growth of a single microbe surviving human immunity system within 24 hours of entering the body. The danger to human beings varies proportionately with the toxicity, potency and growth attributed to a microbe shown in the figure below.

[Bubble chart: x-axis = Potency (0-10), y-axis = Toxicity (0-6). Circles: P(1.2) at potency~4, toxicity~5; Q(2) at potency~2, toxicity~2; R(0.5) at potency~7, toxicity~1; S(4) at potency~5, toxicity~4]

A pharmaceutical company is contemplating the development of a vaccine against the most dangerous microbe. Which microbe should be the target in its first attempt?

A. P     B. Q     C. R     D. S
questions.to_find: which microbe (P, Q, R, or S) poses the greatest danger based on the combined toxicity, potency, and growth

understand.plain: Danger is proportional to the product of three attributes: toxicity (y-axis), potency (x-axis), and growth (represented by the area of the circle, where area = pi * (diameter/2)^2). We need to estimate each microbe's danger score = toxicity x potency x growth_area and identify the maximum. Microbe S has a large circle (diameter 4), moderate-to-high toxicity, and moderate-to-high potency, making it the most dangerous overall.

keywords.explain: The area of each circle, not the diameter, represents growth. Area = pi * r^2 = pi * (d/2)^2. A larger diameter means much larger area since area scales as d^2.
keywords.example: Diameter 4 gives area = pi*(4/2)^2 = 4*pi approx 12.57, whereas diameter 1.2 gives area = pi*(0.6)^2 approx 1.13

keywords.explain: Danger = toxicity x potency x growth_area. All three factors multiply together, so a microbe must be high on all three dimensions to be most dangerous.
When danger varies proportionately with multiple factors, you multiply them. One very high factor alone is not enough if the others are low.
keywords.example: R has high potency (~7) but very low toxicity (~1) and small diameter (0.5), giving a low danger score

no'>dangerous</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A bubble chart where the x-axis is potency and y-axis is toxicity; microbe S has the largest circle (growth diameter 4), positioned at moderate-high potency and toxicity, making it the most dangerous overall.

given.aim: find the microbe with the highest danger score = toxicity x potency x growth_area

terms.meaning: Area = pi*(d/2)^2 where d is the diameter shown in brackets; area scales as d^2 so large diameters dominate
terms.example: S has d=4: area = pi*4 approx 12.57; P has d=1.2: area = pi*0.36 approx 1.13
terms.connects: determines the growth factor in the danger product

terms.meaning: Read from the vertical axis; higher position = more toxic
terms.example: P is near toxicity 5, S is near toxicity 4, Q near 2, R near 1
terms.connects: one of the three multiplied factors

terms.meaning: Read from the horizontal axis; further right = more potent
terms.example: R is near potency 7, S near 5, P near 4, Q near 2
terms.connects: one of the three multiplied factors

terms.meaning: Danger = k * toxicity * potency * area for some positive constant k; comparing ratios is enough to find the maximum
terms.example: We need not compute absolute values, just compare relative products
terms.connects: allows ranking microbes without knowing the constant k

given.plan: Estimate danger score for each microbe as toxicity x potency x pi*(d/2)^2. Since pi cancels in comparisons, compute toxicity x potency x (d/2)^2 for P, Q, R, S and find the maximum.

steps.title: Read chart values for each microbe
steps.apply: From the bubble chart: P: toxicity approx 5, potency approx 4, diameter = 1.2 | Q: toxicity approx 2, potency approx 2, diameter = 2 | R: toxicity approx 1, potency approx 7, diameter = 0.5 | S: toxicity approx 4, potency approx 5, diameter = 4
steps.note: The diameter is explicitly printed inside or next to each circle; the area (not diameter) represents growth.

steps.title: Compute relative danger score = toxicity x potency x (d/2)^2 for each microbe
steps.note: S scores 80, far exceeding P (7.2), Q (4), and R (0.44). The large diameter of S (4 vs others at most 2) contributes a factor of 16 in area compared to diameter 2, making S dominant.

steps.title: Identify the most dangerous microbe
steps.apply: S has the highest danger score (80) by a large margin. The pharmaceutical company should target microbe S first.
steps.note: Even though R has the highest individual potency and P has the highest individual toxicity, neither reaches the combined danger of S due to S's much larger growth (area).

solution.result: Microbe S is the most dangerous, with danger score approximately 80 (vs P=7.2, Q=4, R=0.44). The answer is D. S.

questions.formula_note: Danger is computed as the product of three proportional attributes; since all share the same constant of proportionality, ranking by the raw product of toxicity, potency, and circle area (pi*(d/2)^2) suffices.
